question: What is biodiversity?
answer: The range of different species in an area
accept: the variety of different species in an area
how many different species live in an area
misconceptions: the total number of animals in an area
the number of plants in a field

question: Which field has the HIGHER biodiversity?
answer: A meadow with many different plants and insects
options: A field growing only one crop
A meadow with many different plants and insects
A field of corn full of corn plants
A car park with a few weeds
distractors: a single-crop field is full of plants but supports few species, so its biodiversity is low
a corn field grows only one species, so biodiversity is low even though it is full of plants
very few species can live there, so biodiversity is low

question: Explain why a field growing only one crop has low biodiversity.
answer: The food source is uniform and limited to only one species
so it can support fewer types of insect and animal
accept: only one type of food is available
fewer different species can live there
misconceptions: it has high biodiversity because it is full of plants
more plants always means more biodiversity
the crop counts as many species

question: Why would we expect to find many species of insect in a meadow with high biodiversity?
answer: There are many different plant species providing many different food sources
options: There are many different plant species providing many different food sources
Because the meadow is larger than a corn field
Because insects prefer meadows to all other places
Because there is only one type of plant for them to eat
distractors: size is not the reason; it is the variety of food sources that supports more species
insects are supported by varied food sources, not a simple preference for meadows
a single plant type is a feature of LOW biodiversity, which supports fewer species

question: Give the four reasons why we keep biodiversity high.
answer: Some species are useful (e.g. for medicines)
biodiversity supports our food supply
food webs are affected if a species is lost
it is morally the right thing to do
accept: species can give us medicines
loss of one species harms the whole food web
we have a responsibility to protect the planet
misconceptions: so there are more animals to look at

question: Explain why losing one species can affect many other organisms.
answer: The species is part of a food web
so if one species is reduced there are consequences throughout the food web affecting many organisms
accept: other organisms depend on it for food
food webs are connected
misconceptions: only that one species is affected
losing a species has no wider effect
it only affects the predators that eat it

question: What is meant by classification?
answer: Putting organisms into groups (so we can study them)
accept: sorting organisms into groups
grouping organisms

question: Name the scientist who devised the classification system we mostly use today.
answer: Carl Linnaeus
accept: Linnaeus

question: Which of these is one of the five kingdoms that organisms are sorted into?
answer: Fungi
distractors: insects are a group of invertebrate animals, not a kingdom
vertebrates are a group within the Animal kingdom, not a kingdom themselves
bacteria belong to the Prokaryote kingdom; 'Prokaryotes' is the kingdom name

question: Name the five kingdoms that all organisms are sorted into.
answer: Animals
Plants
Fungi
Prokaryotes
Protoctists
accept: animal kingdom
plant kingdom
fungi kingdom

question: What is a vertebrate?
answer: An animal that has a backbone
accept: an animal with a spine
an animal with a backbone

question: Which sequence of groups do humans belong to, starting from the kingdom?
answer: Animals then vertebrates then mammals
distractors: humans are in the Animal kingdom, not the Plant kingdom
humans have a backbone, so they are vertebrates, not invertebrates

question: Two organisms belong to the same species if they can do what?
answer: Reproduce to produce fertile offspring
accept: breed to make offspring that can also reproduce
make fertile young together
misconceptions: produce any offspring even if infertile

question: A bullfinch and a chaffinch look similar but cannot reproduce to make fertile offspring. Are they the same species? Explain.
answer: No, they are different species
because they cannot reproduce to produce fertile offspring
accept: different species, as their offspring would not be fertile
misconceptions: yes, because they are both finches
yes, because they are the same size

question: A male and female bullfinch look quite different but can breed to make fertile young. What does this tell us?
answer: They are the same species
options: They are different species because they look different
distractors: looking different does not make organisms different species; what matters is fertile offspring
they clearly reproduce, so they are animals
they can breed to make fertile young, so they can reproduce

question: What does it mean for a species to be extinct?
answer: When the last of a species has died and its numbers have reached zero
accept: none of that species are left alive
the species has died out completely
misconceptions: the species has very low numbers

question: What does it mean for a species to be endangered?
answer: A species with very low numbers that is close to extinction
accept: very few left and at risk of dying out
numbers are very low and near zero
misconceptions: the species is increasing in number

question: The number of a pigeon species has fallen from 100,000 to 10. Is this pigeon extinct? Explain your answer.
answer: No, it is not extinct
because some are still alive (it would only be extinct if numbers reached zero); it is endangered
accept: not extinct, it is endangered as numbers are very low but not zero
misconceptions: yes, because the numbers dropped a lot
yes, because there are only a few left
extinct and endangered mean the same thing

question: Explain the difference between an endangered species and an extinct species.
answer: Endangered means very low numbers and close to extinction
extinct means the numbers have reached zero (none are left)
accept: endangered = nearly gone but still alive; extinct = all dead

question: Which of the following is NOT a cause of extinction listed in this topic?
answer: Afforestation
distractors: loss of habitat is a key cause of extinction
introducing new predators can cause a species to die out
competition with better-adapted species can cause extinction

question: List five reasons that can cause organisms to become extinct.
answer: Loss of habitat
new predators
competition
hunting
pollution
accept: habitat destruction
being killed by humans
new species out-competing them

question: Explain how cutting down trees can lead to a reduction in biodiversity.
answer: Cutting down trees destroys the habitats of organisms that live on or in them
so the numbers of these animals decrease
which reduces the range of species in the area (lower biodiversity)
accept: deforestation removes habitats so species die out and biodiversity falls
misconceptions: trees are the only species so biodiversity is unchanged
animals simply move and nothing is lost

question: Give three ways in which humans harm biodiversity.
answer: Deforestation (cutting down forests)
building cities
pollution of air, water and land
accept: cutting down trees
replacing green areas with buildings
releasing pollution from cars and factories

question: Give four ways in which humans can maintain biodiversity.
answer: Breeding programmes
afforestation
conservation of habitats
seed and gene banks
accept: breeding animals in captivity then releasing them
planting new forests
protecting existing habitats
storing seeds or DNA

question: What is afforestation?
answer: Planting a forest where there were no trees before
accept: establishing a forest in an area with no previous tree cover
planting trees where there were none

question: How does a global seed vault help to protect our food supply?
answer: It stores millions of seeds of crops
so that if a disease destroys our food crops we can take the seeds and grow those crops again
accept: seeds can be used to regrow crops after disease wipes them out
misconceptions: seeds cannot be regrown once stored

question: A bird population falls from 3000 individuals to 1200. By how many individuals has the population decreased?
A farmer's field has been used to grow only one type of crop. Suggest how the field could be changed to maintain biodiversity.
answer: Grow many different species of plant in the field
so it can support more different species of insect and animal
accept: plant a variety of plants instead of one crop
turn it into a meadow with mixed plants
plant trees / use afforestation
misconceptions: grow even more of the same crop
leave it exactly as it is

question: A meadow contains 8 species of plant, 6 species of insect and 4 species of bird. How many species are there in total?
answer: 18 species
